skill.name: project-development
skill.description: Guides teams through planning, validating, and building LLM projects with staged pipelines, task-model fit checks, cost estimates, and iteration practices.
skill.summary: Plan and structure LLM project development with pipeline patterns, validation steps, and cost-aware execution.
skill.author: muratcankoylan

security_audit.summary: AI review did not find malicious intent or prompt injection attempts in the skill content. Most high and critical static alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, file names, model names, and documentation examples. The skill includes a runnable Python pipeline template that writes and deletes local files and can be customized to call LLM APIs, so publication should include a clear operational warning.

medium_findings.title: Runnable Template Performs Local File Writes and Deletes
medium_findings.description: The included Python template creates batch directories, writes raw prompts, parsed results, and rendered HTML, and its clean stage deletes generated stage outputs. This is legitimate pipeline behavior, but users should review paths and run it only inside an intended project directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly writes files and deletes selected stage outputs through pathlib. The behavior is explicit and scoped to configured data and output directories, so it is an operational risk rather than evidence of malware.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Content Is Inserted Into LLM Prompts
medium_findings.description: The template formats item title and content directly into the LLM prompt. If users adapt the template for untrusted web or document inputs, those inputs can influence downstream model output unless they add prompt-injection handling and validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The content insertion path is clear, and the template is intended for LLM processing. There is no malicious payload in the skill files, but projects using untrusted inputs need defensive prompt design.

low_findings.title: Static Command-Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick findings correspond to fenced Markdown examples, inline code, or code blocks that describe pipeline structure. I did not find evidence that the skill secretly executes shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation code fences or inline examples, not executable Ruby backticks. The actual Python script does not call subprocess, os.system, eval, or exec.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Reference Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to cited external resources for case studies and references. They are not fetched automatically by the skill or script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in Markdown source and reference sections. No code path was found that automatically sends data to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak-Cryptography Alerts Are Non-Cryptographic Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts map to Markdown syntax, file extensions, model names, or generic text rather than cryptographic functions. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak algorithms being used for security.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed contexts show documentation headings, token estimation, and argparse configuration. They do not implement cryptographic operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Filesystem Mutation
dangerous_patterns.description: The pipeline template creates, overwrites, and deletes generated project files as part of normal operation. Users should run it in a controlled workspace and confirm DATA_DIR and OUTPUT_DIR before using the clean command.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly implemented in the script and can affect local project files. It is bounded to configured paths but still warrants a marketplace warning.

dangerous_patterns.title: Optional External LLM API Integration
dangerous_patterns.description: The template includes commented guidance for replacing the mock response with an OpenAI or Anthropic API call. This is expected for an LLM pipeline, but users should manage credentials and data-sharing policies themselves.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The current script only returns a mock response, but the intended customization path is an external LLM call. Risk depends on how the user adapts the template.

content.value_statement: LLM projects often fail when teams automate before validating task fit, cost, and output structure. This skill provides a practical planning method, pipeline template, and review checklist for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Helps evaluate whether a task fits LLM strengths before automation starts.
Explains a five-stage acquire, prepare, process, parse, and render pipeline pattern.
Provides a Python batch pipeline template with caching, parsing, rendering, cleaning, and cost estimation stages.
Summarizes production case studies on pipeline design, tool reduction, context management, and multi-agent research.
Guides structured prompt design and resilient parsing for model-generated outputs.
Supports cost and scale planning for batch LLM workloads.
limitations: The included Python template is a starting point and requires project-specific data acquisition and API integration.
The mock LLM call does not process real model outputs until users customize it.
Cost examples and model names may need updating for current provider pricing.
The skill does not provide deployment, monitoring, or compliance controls by itself.

use_cases.title: Validate a New LLM Product Idea
use_cases.description: Use the task-model fit checklist and manual prototype step before investing in automation.

use_cases.title: Build a Batch Analysis Pipeline
use_cases.description: Adapt the staged Python template to process many files, cache intermediate outputs, parse responses, and render reports.

use_cases.title: Review Agent Architecture Choices
use_cases.description: Compare single-agent, multi-agent, and reduced-tool designs using the included case studies and cost guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Check Task Fit
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether this project is a good fit for LLM processing. Identify the input, desired output, error tolerance, needed domain knowledge, and reasons to use traditional code instead. Project: [describe project].

prompt_templates.title: Plan Pipeline Stages
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an acquire, prepare, process, parse, and render pipeline for this workload. List each stage output, cache key, retry rule, and failure mode. Workload: [describe data and output].

prompt_templates.title: Estimate Cost and Scale
prompt_templates.prompt: Estimate token usage, model cost, runtime, parallel worker count, and retry buffer for this batch job. Include assumptions and the first measurements I should collect. Batch details: [items, prompt size, expected output].

prompt_templates.title: Audit Pipeline Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this LLM pipeline design for architectural risk. Check task-model fit, cache strategy, parser resilience, prompt injection exposure, file safety, cost controls, and test coverage. Design: [paste architecture].

output_examples.input: A team wants to grade 10,000 support tickets with an LLM.
output: Task fit assessment with risks around inconsistent labels and privacy review.
Recommended staged pipeline with raw ticket capture, prompt generation, model calls, parser checks, and dashboard rendering.
Cost estimate based on sample ticket length, expected output size, retry buffer, and worker count.

output_examples.input: A developer asks how to structure an agent-assisted research project.
output: Recommendation to start with a single-agent prototype and add subagents only for context isolation.
Suggested file layout for input sources, prompts, responses, parsed results, and final reports.
Checklist for structured outputs, parser fallbacks, and manual review samples.

output_examples.input: A lead wants to reduce an overbuilt LLM workflow.
output: Comparison of specialized tools against simpler file and command interfaces.
Questions to identify which constraints help quality and which add maintenance burden.
Iteration plan that removes one layer at a time and measures success rate, latency, and token use.

best_practices: Validate one representative example manually before writing automation.
Keep pipeline stages discrete, idempotent, cacheable, and easy to inspect.
Track token usage, parsing failures, retries, and quality samples during development.
anti_patterns: Building a full pipeline before confirming the model can produce useful outputs.
Combining acquisition, model calls, parsing, and rendering in one opaque script.
Assuming model output will always match the requested format exactly.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete production system?
faq.answer: No. It provides methodology, examples, and a customizable template. You must add real data sources, API calls, tests, and deployment controls.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it when planning an LLM project, evaluating task-model fit, designing batch pipelines, or reviewing agent architecture choices.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is model-agnostic and the marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code support.

faq.question: Does the Python template call an external API by default?
faq.answer: No. The template returns a mock response until users replace the placeholder function with a real LLM API call.

faq.question: What files can the template modify?
faq.answer: By default it writes under data and output directories and can delete generated stage files through the clean command.

faq.question: How should teams handle untrusted input?
faq.answer: Treat source content as untrusted, add prompt-injection defenses, validate parsed output, and review samples before scaling.
